source.title: Neural signatures of arbitration between Pavlovian and instrumental action selection

confidence_rationale: Computational + EEG/fMRI demonstration that arbitration between Pavlovian and instrumental control is dynamically modulated within a single session, and that the modulation is governed by an inferred controllability variable that is itself a separate computation from per-channel reliability. This is the closest existing-literature evidence that >2-channel arbitration with multiple modulating variables (controllability + reliability + Pavlovian bias) is the norm rather than the exception in biological arbitration. Source quality high (Gershman lab, PLOS Comp Bio). Mapping fidelity moderate -- supports REE's MECH-312 multi-variable claim but the specific channel set (Pavlovian + instrumental) is different from REE's (uncertainty + practice + diversity).
failure_signatures: The Pavlovian-vs-instrumental arbitration framework treats Pavlovian responses as bias terms that competes with instrumental control, not as a co-equal arbitration channel. REE's MECH-312 with 3+ channels of equal status is structurally different.
Controllability inference is itself a Bayesian latent-state estimate; REE has not committed to a normative Bayesian framework for V_s or for arbitration weights more generally.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The balance between Pavlovian and instrumental influences on action selection is dynamically arbitrated within a single experimental session. The arbitration is driven by an inferred controllability variable -- the agent's estimate of how much its actions actually influence outcomes. When controllability is inferred to be low, Pavlovian biases dominate; when high, instrumental control dominates. EEG and fMRI signatures of the controllability inference are distinct from per-channel reliability signals, indicating that arbitration is governed by multiple computations operating in parallel.
mapping.ree_translation: REE's MECH-312 needs an arbitration mechanism that can handle multiple channels (3+) modulated by multiple variables (uncertainty + practice maturity + V_s freshness + diversity pressure + simulation-mode tag). Gershman et al 2021 demonstrate the existence of biological arbitration that already does this in the Pavlovian-vs-instrumental case: the controllability variable is an additional modulating signal beyond per-channel reliability, and the arbitration is dynamically updated within a session. This supports the structural premise that multi-variable arbitration is biologically plausible and points to controllability/agency as a candidate ADDITIONAL modulating variable REE has not yet considered explicitly.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Two transfer risks. (1) Pavlovian-vs-instrumental is a special case of arbitration where one channel (Pavlovian) is hard-wired and the other (instrumental) is learned; REE's MECH-312 has multiple learned channels. The arbitration mechanism may differ. (2) Controllability is a specific modulating variable that REE has not yet operationalised; whether it should be added to MECH-312's modulating-variable set is a substrate-design call.
mapping.source_context: Computational model fit to two prior datasets: one EEG, one fMRI, both go/no-go tasks. Re-analysis approach; not new behavioural data.

confidence_components.notes: Computational re-analysis paper. Strong on the principle (multi-variable arbitration with dynamic within-session updating is biologically real); moderate on direct REE applicability because the channel set differs.
